frameworks.name: arousal modulation of cortical state
frameworks.one_line: Subcortical neuromodulatory arousal continuously shapes cortical population activity

frameworks.name: pupil-linked arousal
frameworks.one_line: Pupil size indexes central arousal and tracks regionally specific cortical effects

frameworks.name: excitation-inhibition balance
frameworks.one_line: Aperiodic 1/f component reflects shifts in microcircuit E/I balance

frameworks.name: locus coeruleus neuromodulation
frameworks.one_line: Brainstem neuromodulatory system implicated as driver of arousal signals

core_question: How do moment-to-moment fluctuations in pupil-linked arousal relate to spatially and spectrally specific patterns of cortical population activity across the human brain?
key_assumptions: Pupil size is a reliable peripheral index of central arousal systems
Arousal effects on cortex are heterogeneous across regions and frequency bands rather than uniform
MEG power and aperiodic structure reflect underlying microcircuit dynamics
manipulations: Time-locking cortical activity to peak of spontaneous pupil dilations
Comparison across cortical regions (temporal, lateral frontal, mid-frontal, occipito-parietal)
Spectral decomposition into low (2-8 Hz), intermediate (8-32 Hz), and high (>64 Hz) bands
Separation of periodic spectral peaks from aperiodic 1/f component
key_findings: Low-frequency (2-8 Hz) power decreases in temporal and lateral frontal cortex around pupil dilation peaks
High-frequency (>64 Hz) power increases in mid-frontal regions following the low-frequency decrease
Intermediate-band (8-32 Hz) activity in occipito-parietal cortex shows monotonic and inverted-U relationships with pupil size
Widespread changes in the aperiodic 1/f component co-occur with pupil-linked arousal fluctuations
interpretations: Arousal is not a homogeneous cortical modulator but exerts region- and frequency-specific influences on population activity
Pupil-coupled aperiodic changes index shifts in cortical excitation-inhibition balance, providing a basis for studying arousal modulation of cognition
